title: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Targeted Testing in Renal Disorders
items.content: Random testing for renal disorders is time-consuming and inefficient. A logical approach involves reviewing history, physical examination, and basic laboratory studies to guide targeted tests that identify specific disease processes.
keyPoints: Avoid random testing; it generates a long list of potential diagnoses without clarity.
Use evidence from history, physical exam, and labs for targeted testing.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.title: Role of Complement Cascade in Renal Diseases
items.content: The complement cascade, involving classical, alternative, and lectin pathways, is implicated in renal diseases through dysregulation. It plays a role in inflammatory responses and immune complex deposition, which can affect glomerular, tubular, and vascular structures.
keyPoints: Complement cascade includes classical, alternative, and lectin pathways.
Dysregulation leads to disease via proinflammatory complement proteins.
Immune complex deposition has diagnostic value in renal diseases.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.title: Significance of Complement Component C3
items.content: Complement component C3 is crucial across all complement pathways, yielding proinflammatory effects and aiding immune complex clearance. Its dysregulation can lead to thrombotic microangiopathies.
keyPoints: C3 is central in classical, alternative, and lectin pathways.
Yields C3a and C3b; C3b has further proinflammatory effects.
Dysregulation may cause life-threatening endothelial damage.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES
items.title: Recognizing Nephritic Syndromes and Their Inflammatory Origins
items.content: Nephritis, an inflammatory condition of the kidney, can arise from various causes such as infections, allergic reactions to medications, systemic autoimmune disorders, or toxic exposures. The inflammation may involve multiple organs or be isolated to the kidneys alone.
keyPoints: Inflammation in nephritic syndromes can occur due to infection, medication allergies, systemic autoimmune disorders, or toxins.
Kidney involvement may present with enlargement and point tenderness over the flank; costovertebral angle tenderness may also be observed.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES
items.title: Subclassification of Nephritic Syndromes Based on Inflamed Structures and Progression
items.content: Nephritic syndromes are categorized based on the specific structures within the kidney that are inflamed, such as glomerular, tubulointerstitial, or vascular. Additionally, these syndromes can be classified by their progression timeline: acute, subacute, or chronic.
keyPoints: Nephritic syndromes may involve glomerular, tubulointerstitial, or vascular structures within the kidney.
The inflammatory process in nephritic syndromes can progress acutely, subacutely, or chronically.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES

items.title: Recognizing Acute GN Presentation and Diagnostic Indicators
items.content: Acute glomerulonephritis often presents with low urine output, concentrated urine, and an abnormal urinalysis. Observing a spun urinary sediment is crucial for diagnosing active GN.
keyPoints: Low urine output (oliguria) or complete anuria
Concentrated urine with low sodium content
Spun urinary sediment showing hematuria, proteinuria, cells/clumps of cells, and cellular casts
Red blood cells in urine often dysmorphic due to crossing the glomerular membrane
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Postinfectious Glomerulonephritis Pathophysiology
items.content: Postinfectious GN involves immune complex deposition and complement activation, leading to intense inflammation. It typically follows infections by nephritogenic strains of group A streptococci.
keyPoints: Immune complexes form 10 days to 3 weeks post-infection
Activation of the complement cascade causes decreased circulating C3 levels
Proliferative injury with subendothelial glomerular deposits
Hypocomplementemia due to increased clearance of total hemolytic complement and C3
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Implications of Hypocomplementemia in GN
items.content: Hypocomplementemia, particularly low C3 levels, is a key diagnostic marker for certain types of glomerulonephritis, including postinfectious GN and those associated with infective endocarditis.
keyPoints: Low C3 complement levels indicate active immune complex-mediated GN
Hypocomplementemia can persist even after the infection has resolved
Infective endocarditis may also cause hypocomplementemia despite antibiotic treatment
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing PIGN from Sympharyngitic Hematuria
items.content: Postinfectious glomerulonephritis (PIGN) should be differentiated from sympharyngitic hematuria, which is often related to IgA nephropathy (IgAN). While both conditions can present with gross hematuria, PIGN is associated with low complement levels in the blood, unlike IgAN.
keyPoints: Sympharyngitic hematuria occurs with or shortly after viral pharyngitis and is often linked to IgA nephropathy (IgAN).
PIGN is characterized by low complement levels in the blood, distinguishing it from IgAN where C3 is deposited but complement levels are not typically low.
Clinicians should recognize that as little as 10–20 mL of blood can turn a liter of urine red.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.title: Management and Treatment Strategies for IgA Nephropathy
items.content: Treatment of IgA nephropathy involves managing hypertension and proteinuria, often using anti-angiotensin II inhibitors. Recent advances have led to targeted therapies based on kidney biopsy findings, including complement blockers and B-cell therapies.
keyPoints: IgAN is managed with anti-hypertensive therapy and control of proteinuria, frequently employing anti-angiotensin II inhibitors.
Recent treatments include avacopan (a selective C5a receptor inhibitor) to reduce proteinuria in IgAN nephropathy.
Targeted therapies are based on biopsy findings showing IgA-1 and glomerular deposition of mannan-binding lectin, present in 50% of patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Renal Biopsy in IgAN Management
items.content: Renal biopsy is crucial for diagnosing and managing IgA nephropathy, especially given the rapid GFR decline in patients with high-risk genetic profiles. This has led to more aggressive management strategies.
keyPoints: IgAN can lead to a rapid decline in GFR, particularly in individuals with high-risk APOL1 alleles.
Renal biopsy is encouraged for diagnosis and guiding treatment due to the potential for rapid progression to end-stage kidney disease.
FDA-approved therapies include dual endothelin 1 and angiotensin II receptor antagonists for reducing proteinuria in high-risk IgAN patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis of ANCA-positive Vasculitis
items.content: ANCA-positive vasculitides, such as granulomatous vasculitis and microscopic polyangiitis, are associated with pulmonary and upper respiratory symptoms. They involve the kidney and peripheral vessels targeted by autoantibodies that attract complement, leading to inflammatory vasculitis.
keyPoints: Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) test is diagnostic for pauci-immune disorders of systemic vasculitis.
Anti-proteinase-3 antibodies (cANCA) or anti-myeloperoxidase (pANCA) are specific markers.
Vasculitides may recur subclinically at different times.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.title: Management of Anti-GBM Disease
items.content: Anti-GBM disease presents acutely and requires rapid diagnosis with ANCA, anti-GBM studies, and renal pathology. Treatment involves plasma exchange followed by immunotherapy such as cyclophosphamide or rituximab to preserve kidney function.
keyPoints: Positive anti-GBM test indicates renal-limited disease; may also present with pulmonary hemorrhage in Goodpasture disease.
Anti-GBM antibodies target the α3 chain of Type 4 collagen in the basement membrane.
Plasmapheresis aims to remove all GBM autoantibodies, which can take weeks to months.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.title: Characteristics of Immune Complex Diseases in RPGN
items.content: Immune complex diseases like SLE and cryoglobulinemia can manifest as RPGN with low C3 complement due to immune deposits. IgA vasculitis presents with normal C3 levels, characterized by skin vasculitis and pulmonary hemorrhage.
keyPoints: Low circulating levels of C3 may occur in immune complex diseases like SLE and cryoglobulinemia.
Cryoglobulinemia often has low C4 complement and high rheumatoid factor.
IgA vasculitis presents with normal C3 levels but is associated with galactose-deficient IgA1 in the mesangium.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.title: Early Diagnosis and Management of Acute Allergic Immune Tubulointerstitial Nephritis (ATIN)
items.content: Acute allergic immune tubulointerstitial nephritis often follows exposure to certain drugs, with symptoms developing 1 day to 2 weeks post-exposure. Early diagnosis is crucial due to the potential for rapid and reversible kidney function loss.
keyPoints: Time course: Develops 1 day to 2 weeks after drug exposure
Symptoms include fever, rash, eosinophilia, polyuria, tender kidneys
Associated drugs: NSAIDs, ampicillin, penicillins, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones, biologic cancer drugs (CPIs), PPIs, sulfa drugs
Urinalysis findings: Pyuria, eosinophiluria, activated T lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells, white blood cell casts
Tubular dysfunction signs: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, hypo- or hyperkalemia, hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.title: Drug Associations and Pathophysiology of ATIN
items.content: ATIN is often drug-induced, with common culprits including NSAIDs, ampicillin, and certain antibiotics. PPIs may increase risk when used with CPIs. Noncaseating granulomatous interstitial nephritis can be caused by rifampin and other drugs.
keyPoints: Common ATIN-inducing drugs: NSAIDs, ampicillin, penicillins, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones, CPIs, PPIs, sulfa drugs
PPIs increase risk for ATIN in patients on CPIs
Noncaseating granulomatous interstitial nephritis: Caused by rifampin and other drugs
Caseating granulomas: Associated with Mycobacterium tuberculosis, first appear connected to glomeruli
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.title: Toxic Exposures and Chronic Tubulointerstitial Nephritis
items.content: Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis can result from toxic exposures, such as heavy metals (cadmium, lead, mercury) or chemotherapeutic agents (ifosfamide, platinum compounds), leading to conditions like Fanconi's syndrome and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
keyPoints: Heavy-metal exposure may cause proximal tubule injury and Fanconi's syndrome.
Platinum-containing agents can lead to hypomagnesemia and AKI.
Lithium, analgesics, or chemotherapy may result in nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
Aminoglycosides or amphotericin B exposure is associated with hypokalemia and interstitial disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.title: Chronic Analgesic Use and Kidney Disease
items.content: Prolonged use of analgesics, including NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or combinations like phenacetin, aspirin, and caffeine, can lead to chronic interstitial nephritis. This condition may precede gastrointestinal symptoms and potentially result in uroepithelial cancers if not addressed.
keyPoints: Chronic analgesic use is a common cause of chronic interstitial nephritis.
Patients should be queried about past pain history and GI symptoms.
Discontinuation of drugs is crucial to prevent progression to uroepithelial cancers.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.title: Understanding Microalbuminuria and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Microalbuminuria, defined as urinary loss of 30–300 mg/d of albumin, is a critical marker in diabetic patients for predicting future renal complications. It indicates early glomerular involvement before overt proteinuria develops.
keyPoints: Microalbuminuria (MALB) defines excessive albuminuria in diabetes mellitus with levels of 30–300 mg/d.
Regular screening for microalbuminuria is recommended for diabetic patients to prevent progression to nephrotic syndrome and renal failure.
Subnephrotic albuminuria (<50% glomeruli involvement) vs. nephrotic syndrome (NS, >3.5 g/d of proteinuria).
Microalbuminuria is not detected by urinary dipstick; overt proteinuria indicates higher levels detectable on dipsticks.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Diagnosis of Nephrotic Syndrome
items.content: Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by edema, hypoalbuminemia (<3.5 g/dL), and significant proteinuria (>3.5 g/d). It may present with lipid abnormalities and specific urinary findings.
keyPoints: Defining features of nephrotic syndrome: edema, hypoalbuminemia (<3.5 g/dL), and proteinuria >3.5 g/d.
Associated lipid abnormalities include high low-density lipoprotein, low high-density lipoprotein, and lipiduria.
Urinary findings may include oval fat bodies with Maltese crosses under polarized light.
Differential diagnosis includes distinguishing from heart failure by observing facial edema.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: APOL1 Gene Mutations and Risk of Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
items.content: Patients, particularly those of African ancestry, with APOL1 gene mutations are at increased risk for developing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), especially when triggered by infection or inflammation.
keyPoints: APOL1-mediated kidney disease involves two risk variants of the APOL1 gene.
Gene expression and subsequent damage require a second hit, such as infection or inflammation.
Increased risk of FSGS in patients with APOL1 mutations, particularly those of African ancestry.
Associated conditions include partial nephrectomy, hepatitis C, and predisposition to kidney disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Understanding the Etiology and Presentation of Minimal Change Disease in Nephrotic Syndrome
items.content: Minimal Change Disease (MCD) is a common cause of nephrotic syndrome, especially in children under 6 years old. It can present abruptly with severe hypoalbuminemia and hypercholesterolemia due to podocytopathy. MCD may be idiopathic or secondary to conditions like Hodgkin's lymphoma. In adults, it might also present with acute kidney injury (AKI) due to underlying vascular disease.
keyPoints: MCD is often preceded by upper respiratory infections, allergies, or immunizations.
In children <6 years old, MCD is a frequent cause of nephrotic syndrome.
Gene mutations in nephrin (NPHS1) and podocin (NPHS2) are linked to hereditary NS.
Severe hypoalbuminemia (<2 g/dL) can lead to tissue ischemia and nephrotic crisis with severe abdominal pain.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Membranous Nephropathy in Nephrotic Syndrome
items.content: Membranous nephropathy (MN) is a significant cause of nephrotic syndrome and can be primary or secondary. It is often associated with renal vein thrombosis, especially when severe hypoalbuminemia (<2 g/dL) is present. MN may present with back pain, renal failure, hematuria, increased edema, or pulmonary emboli.
keyPoints: MN can be primary or secondary and is linked to RVT due to urinary loss of antithrombin-3 and plasminogen, as well as hyperfibrinogenemia.
Severe hypoalbuminemia (<2 g/dL) increases the risk of a hypercoagulable state in NS.
RVT may present with back pain, renal failure, hematuria, increased edema, or pulmonary emboli.
MN can be an early presentation in SLE and may result from NSAID use or exposure to drugs like penicillamine, mercury, and gold.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Autoantibodies in Primary Membranous Nephropathy
items.content: Primary membranous nephropathy (MN) is often associated with circulating autoantibodies to the phospholipase A2 receptor (anti-PLA2R), found in 70% of patients. This subgroup tends to have fewer cases of drug-induced MN and a higher likelihood of spontaneous remission.
keyPoints: 70% of primary MN patients have circulating anti-PLA2R autoantibodies.
Anti-PLA2R positive patients are less likely to have drug-induced MN.
Higher chance of spontaneous remission in anti-PLA2R positive patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 3)

items.title: Diabetic Nephropathy and Its Management
items.content: In diabetic nephropathy, proteinuria is responsive to angiotensin II-inhibiting drugs like ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and renin inhibitors. SGLT2 inhibitors improve glucose control and reduce progression by inducing glycosuria.
keyPoints: Proteinuria in diabetic nephropathy responds to angiotensin II-inhibitors (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, renin inhibitors).
SGLT2 inhibitors induce glycosuria, improving glucose control and reducing disease progression.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 3)

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.title: Identifying Hematuria Source: Lower vs. Upper Tract
items.content: Hematuria can originate from any part of the urinary tract, necessitating identification of its source for appropriate management. Flank pain or ureteral colic suggests a lower urinary tract origin like stones or obstructive lesions. In contrast, flank pain without colic may indicate renal capsule stretching due to conditions such as acute obstruction, infection, interstitial nephritis, or glomerular nephritis.
keyPoints: Hematuria with flank pain/ureteral colic suggests lower urinary tract source (e.g., stones).
Flank pain without colic may indicate renal capsule stretching due to acute obstruction, infection, interstitial nephritis, or glomerular nephritis.
Heavy bleeding with clots more characteristic of lower tract but can result from kidney trauma.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.title: Evaluating Hematuria in Adults: When to Seek Urologic Evaluation
items.content: Hematuria of recent onset, particularly noted in individuals aged 40 or older, warrants a urologic evaluation and likely cystoscopic examination. Chronic hematuric conditions may be linked with chronic glomerular diseases such as thin basement membrane disease, hereditary nephritis, or IgA nephropathy (IgAN). Reviewing past medical examinations can help establish the chronicity of hematuria.
keyPoints: Hematuria in individuals aged 40+ should prompt urologic evaluation and cystoscopy.
Chronic hematuric conditions may be associated with chronic glomerular diseases like thin basement membrane disease, hereditary nephritis, or IgAN.
Review past medical exams to determine if hematuria was previously noted for establishing chronicity.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.title: Understanding the BUN-to-Creatinine Ratio in AKI
items.content: The BUN-to-creatinine ratio is crucial for distinguishing between prerenal and intrinsic renal causes of acute kidney injury (AKI). In conditions with enhanced urinary concentration, such as those involving high antidiuretic hormone levels, urea reabsorption increases more than creatinine, leading to a higher BUN-to-creatinine ratio.
keyPoints: BUN-to-creatinine ratio is elevated in prerenal states due to increased urea reabsorption.
Intrinsic renal disease typically shows a urine sodium concentration <20 mEq/L and a urine to plasma creatinine concentration >40.
Differential diagnosis includes conditions like scleroderma, TMA, and effects of vasoconstrictive substances.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.title: Identifying Intrinsic Renal Disease in AKI
items.content: Intrinsic renal disease is characterized by low urinary sodium excretion, concentrated urine, and a urine to plasma creatinine concentration usually >40. This condition can result from damage to the renal tubules, leading to various electrolyte imbalances.
keyPoints: Characteristic findings include low urinary sodium (<20 mEq/L) and high urine to plasma creatinine ratio (>40).
Common causes of intrinsic renal disease include acute tubular necrosis due to sepsis or toxin exposure.
Polyuria and electrolyte imbalances may occur if the renal tubules are damaged.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.title: Diagnosing Postrenal AKI
items.content: Postrenal AKI is often indicated by anuria in cases of complete urinary obstruction, while polyuria with bland sediment suggests incomplete obstruction. Imaging studies like ultrasound can help visualize hydronephrosis, though exceptions exist.
keyPoints: Anuria indicates complete obstruction; polyuria suggests incomplete obstruction with ADH unresponsiveness.
Fractional excretion of sodium is usually >1-2% in postrenal states.
Hydronephrosis may not be visible if imaging is done too early or if there's concurrent volume depletion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.subtopic: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME
items.title: Diagnosis and Characteristics of Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
items.content: Chronic kidney disease is often diagnosed by the presence of small kidneys (<8 cm) on ultrasound, indicating atrophic kidneys with irreversibly low function. However, in conditions like diabetes mellitus (DM), kidneys may appear large despite failure. CKD can still lead to renin and erythropoietin production but results in secondary hyperparathyroidism due to low calcitriol levels.
keyPoints: Small kidneys (<8 cm) on ultrasound suggest atrophic kidneys with irreversibly low function.
Normal kidney length is 10–12 cm.
In CKD, elevated PTH levels indicate secondary hyperparathyroidism due to low calcitriol.
DM accounts for ~50% of CKD patients progressing to end-stage renal disease (ESRD).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME
items.title: Progression and Management of Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (PCKD)
items.content: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease is characterized by the presence of multiple cysts in both kidneys, often detectable by ultrasound by age 30. Rapid progression to kidney failure can occur due to cyst enlargement from fluid secretion and growth of the cyst wall. Tolvaptan, an inhibitor of vasopressin type 2 receptors, can be used to antagonize cyst growth.
keyPoints: PCKD presents with multiple cysts in both kidneys detectable by ultrasound by age 30.
Cyst enlargement is due to fluid secretion and increased growth of the cyst wall.
Tolvaptan inhibits vasopressin type 2 receptors, helping to manage cyst growth.
Risk factors for rapid progression include African-American heritage, obesity, liver abnormalities, and hypertension.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME

items.title: Distinguishing Hereditary Nephritis from Thin Basement Membrane Disease
items.content: Hereditary nephritis, characterized by hematuria and neurosensory hearing loss, involves thin basement membranes. It must be differentiated from the less severe thin basement membrane disease.
keyPoints: Hematuria is a predominant symptom in hereditary nephritis.
Pathological correlate: thin basement membranes.
Distinguish from thin basement membrane disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Ureteral Reflux and its Consequences
items.content: Ureteral reflux involves the ureter's failure to prevent retrograde urine flow during micturition, potentially causing hypertension and secondary FSGS in children.
keyPoints: Abnormal insertion of the ureter into the bladder causes reflux disease.
Can lead to hypertension and secondary FSGS in children.
May be curative with surgical correction or resolve as the child grows.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.title: Ethical Considerations in CKD Management
items.content: In advanced CKD, clinicians must discuss renal replacement therapy options and transplantation timing while ensuring unbiased access to all patients.
keyPoints: eGFR <15–20 mL/min indicates need for discussions on treatment options.
Discuss peritoneal dialysis or hemodialysis preparation.
Ensure equal discussion of transplantation opportunities across populations.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.title: Management of Anemia in CKD and Erythrocytosis in Renal Masses
items.content: In chronic kidney disease (CKD), anemia is common and often managed with erythropoietic stimulating agents aiming for hemoglobin levels greater than 10 g/dL but less than 12 g/dL. However, not all patients with renal conditions have anemia; some may develop erythrocytosis, particularly in cases like UTO, PCKD, renal vascular disease, and renal cell carcinoma.
keyPoints: Anemia management target: hemoglobin >10 g/dL but <12 g/dL
Erythropoietic stimulating agents are effective for anemia in CKD
Not all patients with renal conditions have anemia; some may present with erythrocytosis
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.title: Progression of CKD and Importance of Managing Cardiovascular Risk Factors
items.content: Chronic kidney disease can progress to late stages even after acute inflammation subsides, with inflammatory pathways playing a role in progression. Elevated levels of C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are indicative of this process. Early management of cardiovascular risk factors such as hypertension, smoking, hyperlipidemia, diabetes mellitus, and obesity is crucial.
keyPoints: CKD can progress despite subsiding acute inflammation
Inflammatory pathways (elevated C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate) are involved in CKD progression
Early management of cardiovascular risk factors is essential
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.title: Differentiating Bartter's and Gitelman's Syndromes via Urine Analysis
items.content: Bartter's syndrome is characterized by hypercalciuria and an inability to concentrate urine, while Gitelman's syndrome presents with hypocalciuria and a preserved ability to concentrate urine. These syndromes can be distinguished through urine analysis for solutes regulated by specific transporters.
keyPoints: Bartter's syndrome: hypercalciuria, unable to concentrate urine
Gitelman's syndrome: hypocalciuria, preserved urine concentration ability
Urine analysis helps differentiate between Bartter's and Gitelman's syndromes
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.question: Which renal structure is primarily responsible for initiating the plasma ultrafiltrate?
items.explanation: The excretory system begins with a plasma ultrafiltrate from renal cortical glomeruli.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.question: What is a hallmark of kidney diseases that begin primarily with glomerular dysfunction?
options: Disorders of dilution and concentration of urine
items.explanation: Kidney diseases that begin primarily with glomerular dysfunction have albuminuria as the hallmark.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.question: What is a common screening test recommended for patients with diabetes mellitus?
items.explanation: Routine screening for microalbuminuria and an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) is recommended in diabetes mellitus.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.question: Which complement component, when activated by C3 convertase, yields both C3a and C3b?
items.explanation: In the presence of C3 convertase, C3 yields C3a and C3b.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the triad associated with certain nephritic syndromes?
items.explanation: The triad consists of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and end-organ dysfunction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES
items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT mentioned as being associated with immune complex diseases in nephritic syndromes?
items.explanation: Lupus, syphilis, and cryoglobulinemia are mentioned as immune complex diseases associated with nephritic syndromes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES
items.question: Which of the following is a common feature in patients with nephritic inflammation?
items.explanation: Inflammation with enlargement of the kidneys is a common feature in nephritic inflammation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES

items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical manifestation associated with glomerulonephritis?
items.explanation: Glomerulonephritis is associated with volume expansion, often manifesting as peripheral edema.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.question: What type of hematuria is typically observed in nephritic syndromes?
items.explanation: Nephritic syndromes usually show microscopic hematuria, which is invisible to the naked eye.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.question: In postinfectious glomerulonephritis, what immune response occurs after infection with nephritogenic strains of group A streptococci?
options: Direct invasion of renal tissue by bacteria
Activation of T-cells against kidney cells
Production of anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies
items.explanation: Postinfectious glomerulonephritis involves the formation of antibody-bacterial antigen complexes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a pathologic feature seen in postinfectious glomerulonephritis?
items.explanation: Postinfectious glomerulonephritis is characterized by 'bloodless capillaries' due to cellular proliferation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.question: Which immunoglobulin is primarily involved in the pathogenesis of IgA nephropathy?
items.explanation: IgA nephropathy (IgAN) is caused by mesangial deposition of C3 binding to excess galactose-deficient IgA-1 immunoglobulin.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.question: What distinguishes postinfectious glomerulonephritis (PIGN) from infection-associated GN in terms of complement levels?
options: Only PIGN has low complement levels
Only infection-associated GN has low complement levels
items.explanation: Though C3 complement is deposited in both PIGN and IgAN, only PIGN is associated with low complement levels in the blood.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of synpharyngitic hematuria?
items.explanation: Synpharyngitic hematuria frequently produces a heavier excretion of red blood (gross hematuria).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.question: Which treatment has been approved by the U.S. FDA for reducing proteinuria in adults with primary IgAN at high risk for rapid disease progression?
options: Oral dual endothelin 1 and angiotensin II receptor antagonist
items.explanation: The U.S. FDA has approved an oral dual endothelin 1 and angiotensin II receptor antagonist for this purpose.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.question: Which autoantibodies are diagnostic for pauci-immune disorders of systemic vasculitis?
options: Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) including anti-proteinase-3 (cANCA) and anti-myeloperoxidase (pANCA)
items.explanation: ANCAs, particularly cANCA and pANCA, are diagnostic for pauci-immune disorders of systemic vasculitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.question: What is a key distinction between ANCA-positive vasculitis and anti-GBM disease?
options: ANCA-positive vasculitides may recur, whereas anti-GBM disease usually presents as a single acute fulminant episode
Anti-GBM disease is associated with pulmonary hemorrhage only
Both conditions are characterized by low C3 complement levels
ANCA-positive diseases do not involve the kidneys
items.explanation: A key distinction is that ANCA-positive vasculitides may recur, while anti-GBM disease typically presents acutely and fulminantly.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.question: In which condition are both anti-GBM antibodies and ANCA present with pathology showing pauci-immune disease?
options: A disorder where ANCA exposes the GBM epitope
items.explanation: Both anti-GBM and ANCA can be present in a condition with pauci-immune pathology, where ANCA may expose the GBM epitope.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.question: Which immune complex disease associated with RPGN often presents with low C4 complement and high rheumatoid factor?
items.explanation: Cryoglobulinemia often presents with low C4 complement and high rheumatoid factor.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is typically associated with acute allergic immune tubulointerstitial nephritis (ATIN) caused by certain drugs?
items.explanation: Fever, rash, and eosinophilia are typical symptoms associated with ATIN due to certain drugs.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.question: Which urinalysis finding is most characteristic of acute allergic immune tubulointerstitial nephritis (ATIN)?
options: Pyuria and eosinophiluria with white blood cell casts
items.explanation: The most characteristic urinalysis findings in ATIN are pyuria, eosinophiluria, and white blood cell casts.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.question: Which of the following drugs is least likely to cause acute tubulointerstitial nephritis (ATIN)?
items.explanation: While NSAIDs can cause heavy glomerular proteinuria, they are atypical for causing ATIN.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of chronic interstitial nephritis due to prolonged use?
options: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or combinations of phenacetin, aspirin, and caffeine
items.explanation: Prolonged use of analgesics such as NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or combinations like phenacetin, aspirin, and caffeine is a common cause of chronic interstitial nephritis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.question: Which condition may develop in patients with hypercalcemia or hyperoxaluria due to calcifications within the renal parenchyma?
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia or hyperoxaluria can lead to nephrocalcinosis, characterized by calcifications within the renal parenchyma.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of chronic interstitial nephritis?
items.explanation: Chronic bacterial pyelonephritis is not a common cause of chronic renal failure as it rarely affects both kidneys.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.question: Which condition is defined by urinary loss of 30–300 mg/d of microalbuminuria?
items.explanation: Microalbuminuria (MALB) defines excessive albuminuria in diabetes mellitus.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: What is the minimum level of proteinuria that characterizes nephrotic syndrome?
items.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by proteinuria >3.5 g/d.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following can be detected as oval fat bodies under polarized light in nephrotic syndrome?
items.explanation: Oval fat bodies containing cholesterol monohydrate crystals appear as Maltese crosses under polarized light in nephrotic syndrome.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of nephrotic syndrome compared to heart failure?
items.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome presents with facial, eyelid, and periorbital swelling, unlike heart failure which presents with orthopnea.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with nephrotic syndrome in young children under 6 years of age?
items.explanation: Minimal change disease is frequently seen in young children under 6 years of age with nephrotic syndrome.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: What is a characteristic finding associated with rapid-onset hypoalbuminemia in nephrotic syndrome?
items.explanation: Muehrcke's lines are horizontal linear white lines in the nail bed associated with rapid-onset hypoalbuminemia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of membranous nephropathy (MN) in nephrotic syndrome?
items.explanation: MN in nephrotic syndrome is often associated with renal vein thrombosis due to urinary loss of antithrombin-3 and hyperfibrinogenemia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT commonly associated with membranous nephropathy (MN)?
items.explanation: While diabetic nephropathy can coexist with MN, it is not commonly associated as a primary cause of MN.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 3)

items.question: Which type of drug is effective in reducing proteinuria in diabetic nephropathy?
items.explanation: Angiotensin II-inhibiting drugs, including angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors and angiotensin II receptor blockers, are effective in reducing proteinuria.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 3)

items.question: What is a potential consequence of poorly controlled diabetes on renal function?
items.explanation: In poorly controlled diabetes, increased blood pressure and renal plasma flow lead to increased GFR in single nephrons that are spared.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 3)

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.question: Which condition is more likely associated with flank pain without colic?
options: A stone or obstructing lesion in the lower urinary tract
Swelling of the kidney and stretching of the renal capsule
items.explanation: Flank pain without colic suggests conditions like acute urinary obstruction or kidney infection, which involve swelling of the kidney.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.question: What should be considered in patients with acute flank pain and hypertension?
options: Occlusive vascular event from an embolus or clot
items.explanation: In patients with acute flank pain and hypertension, an occlusive vascular event should be considered to avoid missing a serious condition.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.question: What is the most likely cause of hematuria in patients who engage in strenuous exercise?
items.explanation: Strenuous exercise can lead to hemoglobinuria or myoglobinuria, which should be ruled out by retesting after rest.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with an elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio due to enhanced urinary concentration?
options: Prerenal AKI due to low perfusion states
Intrinsic renal disease causing acute tubular necrosis
Postrenal obstruction with incomplete urine flow
Disorder with high antidiuretic hormone (ADH) levels
items.explanation: Enhanced urinary concentration due to high ADH leads to increased reabsorption of urea and water, elevating the BUN-to-creatinine ratio.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.question: What is a characteristic finding in urine for intrinsic renal disease?
options: Low urinary sodium concentration and urine to plasma creatinine concentration >40
items.explanation: Intrinsic renal disease is characterized by low urinary sodium concentration and a urine to plasma creatinine ratio greater than 40.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause acute tubular necrosis?
options: Postrenal obstruction with incomplete urine flow
items.explanation: Acute tubular necrosis can be caused by gram-negative sepsis, hemodynamic collapse, and hemoglobinuria.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.question: In postrenal AKI with anuria, what is the likely cause?
items.explanation: Anuria in postrenal AKI suggests a complete obstruction to the flow of urine.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.subtopic: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME
items.question: Which finding on renal ultrasound is indicative of chronic kidney disease (CKD)?
items.explanation: Small kidneys, <8 cm, are likely atrophic with irreversibly low function and indicative of CKD.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME
items.question: Which condition is a treatable form of hypertension that does not progress to renal failure?
options: Renal arterial disease due to fibromuscular dysplasia
items.explanation: Renal arterial disease due to fibromuscular dysplasia is a treatable form of hypertension that does not progress to renal failure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME
items.question: What is the first manifestation of autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (PCKD) in some patients?
items.explanation: The first manifestation of PCKD may be a cerebral hemorrhage due to brain aneurysms present in some patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME

items.question: Which condition must be distinguished from hereditary nephritis due to its less severe nature and similar presentation of hematuria?
items.explanation: Hereditary nephritis must be distinguished from thin basement membrane disease, which is less severe and also presents with hematuria.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.question: What congenital kidney abnormality can lead to secondary FSGS due to low nephron numbers?
items.explanation: Horseshoe kidneys, due to low nephron numbers, can result in secondary FSGS.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary functional abnormality in ureteral reflux?
options: The ureter fails to close off during bladder contraction
The ureter detaches from the kidney
items.explanation: In ureteral reflux, the ureter cannot prevent retrograde flux of urine during micturition because it fails to close off upon bladder contraction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.question: What is a debated factor in calculating eGFR using the MDRD equation?
items.explanation: The inclusion of corrective factors for ethnicity and race has been debated in calculating eGFR using the MDRD equation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically associated with chronic kidney disease (CKD)?
items.explanation: While erythrocytosis can occur in certain renal conditions, it is not typically associated with CKD.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.question: What is the typical urine output for patients with CKD due to their inability to concentrate urine?
items.explanation: Patients with CKD have an obligate urine output of approximately 2 L due to their inability to concentrate urine.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and volume depletion?
items.explanation: Bartter's syndrome is characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and volume depletion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause polyuria due to inappropriate free water losses insensitive to ADH?
items.explanation: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus is characterized by polyuria due to inappropriate free water losses insensitive to ADH.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.statement: The urethra is responsible for storing urine.
items.explanation: The bladder, not the urethra, stores urine.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.statement: A positive feedback loop in the alternative complement pathway can lead to life-threatening endothelial damage.
items.explanation: An unsuppressed proinflammatory cycle in the alternative complement pathway could lead to life-threatening endothelial damage.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES
items.statement: Serum complement levels are typically elevated in immune complex diseases.
items.explanation: Serum complement is usually low in these immune complex diseases.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES
items.statement: Nephritic syndromes can be classified based on the inflamed structures within the kidney.
items.explanation: Nephritic syndromes are subdivided depending on the inflamed structures, such as glomerular, tubulointerstitial, and vascular.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 2) | NEPHRITIC SYNDROMES

items.statement: In postinfectious glomerulonephritis, circulating immune complexes activate the complement cascade.
items.explanation: The deposition of antibody-bacterial antigen complexes activates the complement cascade in postinfectious glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.statement: Postinfectious glomerulonephritis is commonly associated with hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia is not a typical feature of postinfectious glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.statement: IgA nephropathy is most commonly associated with IgM deposition in the glomeruli.
items.explanation: IgA nephropathy involves mesangial deposition of C3 binding to excess galactose-deficient IgA-1, not IgM.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with RPGN can develop global glomerulosclerosis if >50% of glomeruli are affected.
items.explanation: When >50% of glomeruli are affected in RPGN, it usually leads to global glomerulosclerosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.statement: People with hereditary nephritis are at risk of developing anti-GBM disease.
items.explanation: People with hereditary nephritis do not develop anti-GBM disease unless they receive a kidney containing the missing antigen.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.statement: The goal of plasmapheresis in anti-GBM disease is to remove all GBM autoantibodies.
items.explanation: Plasmapheresis aims to remove all GBM autoantibodies, which may take weeks to months.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.statement: Patients with acute allergic immune tubulointerstitial nephritis (ATIN) often experience rapid and potentially reversible loss of kidney function.
items.explanation: Early diagnosis is imperative due to the potential for rapid and reversible kidney function loss in ATIN.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.statement: Protein secreted by the proximal tubule (β2-microglobulinuria) is indicative of glomerular disease.
items.explanation: β2-Microglobulinuria indicates tubular disease, not glomerular disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.statement: Lithium use can lead to nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and chronic interstitial nephritis.
items.explanation: Prolonged lithium use is associated with both nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and chronic interstitial nephritis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.statement: Acute pyelonephritis in a solitary kidney or transplanted kidney rarely causes AKI.
items.explanation: Acute pyelonephritis in a solitary kidney or transplanted kidney may cause acute kidney injury (AKI).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS

items.statement: Microalbuminuria is detectable by urinary dipstick protein analysis.
items.explanation: Microalbuminuria is beneath detection by urinary dipstick protein analysis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.statement: Nephrotic syndrome can be associated with lipid abnormalities.
items.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome is often associated with high low-density lipoprotein, low high-density lipoprotein, and lipiduria.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.statement: Nephrotic syndrome can present with acute kidney injury primarily due to underlying vascular disease and hypoalbuminemia.
items.explanation: In adults, nephrotic syndrome may present with acute kidney injury because of the underlying vascular disease and hypoalbuminemia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 2)

items.statement: Diabetic nephropathy is typically associated with small kidneys on ultrasound in advanced stages.
items.explanation: In diabetic nephropathy, heavy proteinuria or large kidneys are often noted on ultrasound in advanced stages.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 2)

items.statement: SGLT2 inhibitors improve glucose control and reduce the rate of progression in diabetic nephropathy.
items.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors create glycosuria, improve glucose control, and have been shown to slow disease progression.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 3)

items.statement: All patients with membranous nephropathy should be exhaustively worked up for underlying neoplastic diseases.
items.explanation: It is not the rule to work up every patient with MN exhaustively for cancer, though a careful history and examination are important.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 3)

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.statement: Hematuria associated with flank pain is more characteristic of a lower urinary tract source.
items.explanation: Hematuria with flank pain suggests upper urinary tract issues like kidney stones or infections.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.statement: Chronic hematuric conditions may be associated with chronic glomerular disease.
items.explanation: Conditions like thin basement membrane nephropathy and IgAN can cause chronic hematuria.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.statement: In prerenal AKI, the fractional excretion of sodium is usually >1-2%.
items.explanation: Prerenal AKI typically presents with a low fractional excretion of sodium due to increased reabsorption.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.statement: COVID-19 patients requiring ventilators often develop multiorgan failure, including AKI.
items.explanation: During the early phase of the COVID-19 pandemic, many patients on ventilators developed multiorgan failure, including AKI.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.subtopic: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME
items.statement: In CKD, the parathyroid gland can raise PTH levels to very high values.
items.explanation: Very elevated PTH levels likely favor a diagnosis of CKD due to secondary hyperparathyroidism.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME
items.statement: In CKD, calcitriol levels are typically high.
items.explanation: Low calcitriol in CKD leads to secondary hyperparathyroidism.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND THE UREMIC SYNDROME

items.statement: Surgical correction of ureteral reflux is always curative.
items.explanation: While surgical correction may be curative, the child might outgrow the condition in adulthood.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.statement: The CKD-EPI equation includes race as a parameter for estimating GFR.
items.explanation: The CKD-EPI equation excludes race as a parameter.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.statement: Anemia in CKD patients is always due to iron deficiency or decreased erythropoietin.
items.explanation: Other causes of anemia should be investigated as it may not always be due to iron deficiency or decreased erythropoietin.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.statement: Bartter's syndrome is associated with hypercalciuria.
items.explanation: Bartter's syndrome is indeed associated with hypercalciuria.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.assertion: Immune complex deposition is specific to kidney diseases.
items.reason: Though immune complex deposition is not specific to the kidney, its presence has great diagnostic and mechanistic value for many forms of renal disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because immune complex deposition is not specific to the kidney.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)
items.assertion: Thrombotic microangiopathies can be caused by an unsuppressed proinflammatory cycle in the complement pathway.
items.reason: An unsuppressed proinflammatory cycle could lead to life-threatening endothelial damage, which underlies thrombotic microangiopathies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease (part 1)

items.assertion: Early diagnosis and treatment are critical in progressive destructive conditions like glomerulonephritis.
items.reason: Treatment for acute reversible disease should be instituted as quickly as possible to prevent chronic kidney failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early diagnosis is critical.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.assertion: In nephritic syndromes, gross hematuria is common.
items.reason: The process of urinary erythrocytes crossing the glomerular membrane barrier causes red blood cells to become misshapen or dysmorphic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; it is unusual for nephritic syndromes to show gross hematuria. The reason is true but does not support the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 1)

items.assertion: IgA nephropathy is a common cause of renal failure worldwide.
items.reason: It is caused by the mesangial deposition of C3 binding to excess galactose-deficient IgA-1 immunoglobulin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Synpharyngitic hematuria is associated with low complement levels in the blood.
items.reason: It occurs simultaneously with viral pharyngitis and is related to IgA nephropathy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because synpharyngitic hematuria is not associated with low complement levels; it is related to IgA nephropathy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 2)

items.assertion: SLE can present with pulmonary hemorrhage.
items.reason: Certain immune complex diseases like SLE and cryoglobulinemia may manifest as RPGN with pulmonary hemorrhage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.assertion: IgA vasculitis is associated with low serum levels of C3 complement.
items.reason: The C3 complement is detected on kidney biopsy in IgA vasculitis, but it does not correlate with low serum levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; IgA vasculitis does not have low serum levels of C3 complement.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (part 3)

items.assertion: APOL1-mediated kidney disease is triggered by infection or inflammation.
items.reason: Gene expression occurs only when a second hit of infection or inflammation triggers damage to podocytes and glomerular endothelial cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.assertion: Overflow proteinuria reliably indicates glomerular damage.
items.reason: The filtration of light chains may cause glomerular damage and albuminuria but does not reliably indicate it due to their charge and molecular weight allowing them to freely cross the glomerular barrier.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason correctly explains why overflow proteinuria does not reliably indicate glomerular damage.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · PROTEINURIC STATES AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES
items.assertion: Hematuria of recent onset in the fourth decade or beyond should undergo urologic evaluation.
items.reason: This age group is at risk for conditions like bladder cancer, which requires cystoscopic examination.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are true; older adults with new-onset hematuria need evaluation to rule out serious causes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · HEMATURIA AND LOWER URINARY TRACT SYNDROMES

items.assertion: Acute tubular necrosis can result from exposure to nephrotoxic drugs.
items.reason: Nephrotoxic drugs like cisplatin and aminoglycosides cause intratubular obstruction leading to acute tubular injury.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.assertion: Hypercalcemia is a common cause of intrinsic renal disease.
items.reason: Hypercalcemia leads to decreased perfusion pressure and GFR while stimulating sodium and water reabsorption.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hypercalcemia causes prerenal AKI by vasoconstriction, not intrinsic renal disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY

items.assertion: In CKD, the eGFR is determined by stable serum creatinine.
items.reason: The equation uses clinical trial data to compare the subject's value to actual GFR measurements.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why eGFR is based on stable serum creatinine.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.assertion: Ethnicity-based corrections in GFR calculations have been universally accepted.
items.reason: These corrections are intended to provide more accurate eGFR values for different populations.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ethnicity-based corrections have faced considerable objection and are not universally accepted.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with CKD often experience nocturia.
items.reason: CKD patients lose their ability to concentrate urine, leading to an obligate urine output of approximately 2 L.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES

items.assertion: Gitelman's syndrome is associated with hypocalciuria.
items.reason: Gitelman's syndrome results from a defect in the thiazide-sensitive sodium-chloride transporter.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease or Urinary Tract Disease · Thrombotic Microangiopathies (part 2) | RENAL MASSES
